desc:
This paddock attaches to a small stable and one room house, all contained within
the paddock fence. The trainer who calls this place his home truly lives with
his animals because no doors separate the individual structures and man and
animal alike can come and go through each as they please. So at best, the fence
could be called the walls of this home with very little for a roof. In the
middle of the paddock, large barrels sit in a spaced out row and are used to
train horses how to be ridden and men how to ride.
name: Animal Paddock

desc:
A great expanse of wild grass, spreading for many miles into the distance,
starts in this almost forgotten spot beside the high thorny hedge that stands to
the west. The ground descends noticeably towards the southeast, forming a deep
depression where grass seems to grow even thicker, whereas, directly south from
here, a steep embankment rises over the green grasslands, and behind it, you can
distinguish the rounded tops of some ancient hills.
name: Outskirts of the Man-high Grass Fields

desc:
You are now immersed amidst a vast green cloud of tangled vegetation. Advance
becomes arduous, yet not impossible, as the weeds stick to you, and you can only
set the thick blades aside with great difficulty. As you step onwards, the space
you leave disappears as the immense mass of grass fills it, closing
suffocatingly around you, reducing your vision to a few paces, and threatening
to engulf you.
name: Surrounded by the High Grass

desc:
All sort of shoots grow together in this area. Some look very old, and rough to
the touch, standing as high as a man or even higher, and their colour is
faded-green, almost ochre; some grow extremely long and thin, formed by many
flexible and resistant fibres, and these ones have very sharp edges; others grow
short and thick, and they seem to coil around the base of the higher ones; and
there are even others that stand slender and light, with smooth borders and a
deep-green colour. They all crowd around you in utter disorder, as if
quarrelling for every inch of the ground.
name: High, Wild Grass

desc:
The profuse grass here is crowded tightly together, as if fighting desperately
for some living space in which to grow. To the west, it seems a bit lighter, and
a sweet smell comes floating in the air from there, while north from here, a
group of old trees stand dispersed along the plains. But to the east and south,
the grass only becomes thicker and thicker, and the stalks grow higher, like an
impenetrable grassy forest that threatens to swallow you.
name: Dense Grass Fields

desc:
Walking through the grass dotted in flowers, the deep smell of honey emanates
from the ground and blunts your senses, filling them up. This is a very strong
and sickly-sweet smell, even suffocating, to the point that you can almost feel
the sugary lumps sticking along your throat. For this is a land of marked
features, from the penetrating odour of the sweet nectar, to the utterly intense
green of the grasslands, everything here is on its purest, almost wildest state.
name: Honey-smelling Pastures

desc:
You find yourself at the northern edge of a wide spread of grass, as dense and
high as there probably doesn't grow anywhere else in Arda. Great deer with dark,
ruddy furs find their perfect natural habitat in this area which is rich in food
and hiding places. The roughness of the deer's coats protects them from cuts,
scrapes and scratches, and their big, twisted antlers stick out over the shoots,
half-hidden among the deep vegetation.
name: Border of the Red Deer Lands

desc:
Enormous shoots, shaking in the wind, wave to and fro heavily, harmlessly
hitting you once and again as you walk by them. This huge tangle of sprigs
appears to extend all over, but mainly southwards, where the giant blades crowd
so densely that they threaten to engulf you. On the contrary, grass grows lower
and lighter to the north, where a flat hill with wide slopes is silhouetted
against the horizon.
name: Dense, High Grass

desc:
You are now at the northern border of an area where grass grows high and dense
beyond imagination, forming a framework of rough, fibrous shoots intertwining in
a mess. Further southwards, vegetation becomes even denser, if possible, taking
the shape of a deep forest, like a little grassy copy of the sinister Mirkwood
forest. Advance through the stems is slow and laborious, and somewhat
frustrating, the grass closing immediately around you as soon as you put it
aside.
name: Boundaries of a Sea of Grass

desc:
Lost in the middle of the great expanse of wild grass, this still spot is the
perfect refuge for the red deer herds. In some places, they have rooted out the
shortest sprigs with their powerful mandibles, opening holes in the lower part
of the grassy tangle in which to shelter from the icy winters or the pouring
spring rains. In other places, they have nibbled the weeds, clearing the
thickness, or dug out shallow hollows with their strong hoofs, where they can
lie to rest.
name: The Resting Place

desc:
You are now immersed amidst a vast green cloud of tangled vegetation. Advance
becomes arduous, yet not impossible, as the weeds stick to you, and you can only
set the thick blades aside with great difficulty. As you step onwards, the space
you leave disappears as the immense mass of grass fills it, closing
suffocatingly around you, reducing your vision to a few paces, and threatening
to engulf you.
name: Surrounded by the High Grass

desc:
You are now immersed amidst a vast green cloud of tangled vegetation. Advance
becomes arduous, yet not impossible, as the weeds stick to you, and you can only
set the thick blades aside with great difficulty. As you step onwards, the space
you leave disappears as the immense mass of grass fills it, closing
suffocatingly around you, reducing your vision to a few paces, and threatening
to engulf you.
name: Surrounded by the High Grass

desc:
A thick layer of weeds have taken possession of what once was cultivated ground.
Beneath the scrub, you can still catch a glimpse of the brown earth, and even
figure out the lines of the furrows. The grass crowds around the plots, but only
tufts and bushes grow inside them, thus making them look like unyielding
bastions in the middle of the infinity of the grasslands.
name: Abandoned Field

desc:
Situated in the middle of the eastern hillside, a small round building stands in
front of you. Its circular wall is made out of irregular, many-sized, and
roughly-cut stones, as if the structure had been hastily built, and they have
hardly endured the weather and the years: some of them have fallen off and lie
scattered on the ground or down the slope, while broad cracks and holes open
their way through the wall. A tiny, worn out wooden door that stands with great
difficulty on its hinges leads inside, whilst an earthy path, starting off at
the entrance, goes away up the winding hillside.
name: In Front of the Half-ruined Hobbit Mill

desc:
A wide, wet, earthy track comes down from the top of the hill, taking a long way
around along this southern hillside, and then setting out for the old
half-ruined building that stands in the middle of the hillside eastwards. Weeds
and other plants have partially covered it, a clear sign that the path has seen
few travellers for years, but the route is still distinguishable. The way
zigzags to detour every remotely steep incline; no slopes to climb for the
hobbits that traced it long ago.
name: The Path to the Old Mill

desc:
The monotonous flatness of the plains seems to be slightly altered by two small
hills standing to the north, that are merely a little unevenness of the ground.
Their gentle-sloping hillsides fade into the grassland, as if melting with it.
But a swallow valley runs between them and, on its bottom, a group of houses
gather around a small circle, forming a tiny village. Their inhabitants are well
accustomed to the hard life in the Vales, the harsh climate and the constant
threat from the Mountains. They are simple men and women, but proud and noble,
and always loyal to their allies.
name: South of a Small Village

desc:
A soft slope rises northwards to where an old, tumbledown-looking building with
walls made out of rough logs and a thatched roof still stands rather
precariously. A bit eastwards, in a small valley between two hills, thin columns
of white smoke climb into the sky from a little village with hardly a dozen
huts. Down the hillside, to the south, a group of ancient trees seem to
withstand the advance of the vast grasslands that extend all around up to the
horizon and beyond.
name: A Wet Hillside

desc:
In this section of the meadow, all kind of flora blooms among the grass stems.
Large and small flowers of many different colours with intense scents abound,
their petals taking every imaginable shape. But there are also other plants:
thick, short, rough stalks, bulbous tubers, leaves sharp as blades or even
thorny plants. They all grow dispersed among the green grassy background, as in
a small vegetal sanctuary, or perhaps a garden for Yavanna's delight.
name: Fertile Plains

desc:
You are now in the middle of the vast expanse of grass that covers practically
all the ground of these vales, like a great crowd of tiny, green-uniformed
soldiers flanking the Great River as it marches past them towards the seas.
Sometimes, when the wind rises - the soft inland wind, as icy in winter as
torrid and dry in summer - the tall stalks swing all in harmony, taking the
shape of wide waves running along the prairie, like a flag waving in the air,
but a limitless one.
name: Fresh Grass Fields

desc:
A number of trees with worn, grey trunks and bent branches stand dispersed along
the grassland. Perhaps one day the huge Mirkwood forest, still free from the
Dark Shadow, extended up to this spot, and these few trees are the last trace of
its presence. But nowadays, the grassy plains have totally surrounded and
isolated them. When the wind rises and blows strong throughout the plains, and
the undulating grass blades resemble the restless ocean, then the tree tops
flutter heavily, like the canvas of the great vessels that sail the seas, and
the trunks seem to stand straight like strong masts. But when the wind ceases to
blow, everything becomes calm and the illusion fades.
name: Some Scattered Trees

desc:
Although the soft swinging of the fresh, deep-green grassy stems gives them a
fragile, delicate look, reality is way different. Winters are extremely tough
and cold, frosts are common, and dew greets the sun every dawn as it rises over
the shadowy Mirkwood forest. Summers, on the contrary, are really hot, almost
torrid. Moreover, most of the animals depend on the juicy grass for their
survival. Thus, behind their weak appearance, the sprigs grow strong and
fibrous, with sharp edges and mighty, firm roots. From the most robust beorning
to the smallest grasshopper, every inhabitant of these lands knows how tough
life is over here.
name: Wild Plains

desc:
Some miles northwards, the top of a low hill rises heavily over the grasslands,
its wide green slopes spreading all around, as if the hill had been wildly
smashed by the hammer of a giant, or perhaps scattered under the march of a huge
army. Here the hillsides incline shyly, almost imperceptibly, as if reluctant to
leave the broad prairie. Only near the top do the slopes become slightly
steeper.
name: At the Foot of a Green Hill

desc:
A high and impenetrable hedge delimits this large farmhouse, whose only way in
is apparently the broad wooden gate that breaks the hedge directly to the west.
The ground seems to descend slightly southeastwards, where there stand a number
of outbuildings and a long house a bit further. A shapely, wide, grassy track
starts at the gate and sets out towards there, going down the meadow, which
appears to be mostly covered with gardens full of lively- coloured flowers, and
dotted with a few old oaks here and there.
name: Inside the Farmstead

desc:
A bend in the high hedge that surrounds this great farmhouse suggests that you
are now at its northwestern corner. The thorny wall towers quite high over a
man's head. Small, dark leaves with pointed spikes along their edges, and what
seem twisted stems with prickles all over, intertwine so densely that no little
bit of light can get through the tangled barrier. In contrast with it, the vivid
garden, that shines in a deep green background sprinkled with the many loud
colours of the flowers, extends to the very foot of the wall, as if totally
unaware of it.
name: Gardens by the Hedge

desc:
Tens and tens, or perhaps hundreds of huge beehives disposed in rows fill this
part of the farmstead. They all seem exactly the same, with their golden
bell-shaped tops made of straw. A deafening noise fills the air, due to the
unceasing drone of the very many bees. And such bees! For their bulky size and
extremely intense colours, it is clear that they are uncommon specimens, but
they are not less hard-working because of that, and so they do their utmost to
produce honey and wax for their master.
name: The Giant Beehives

desc:
This wide path goes down through the hillside, across the green spreads and the
flower patches, heading towards a group of small wooden buildings in the south,
and, beyond them, down into a small courtyard in front of a low, long, wooden
house. In the other direction, it bends softly westwards, leading to the broad
gate in the hedge, but not entering the beehive fields that lie to the north,
from where you can hear a loud noise. Animals seem to wander freely through
pastures and gardens, but always alert to the presence of foreigners.
name: A Track through the Gardens

desc:
The gentle slope descends from the west levels to this area, forming a wide flat
expanse, as if, long ago, a giant among giants had sat to rest on the hillside.
A large pool fills the terrace, and its quiet waters look still, almost
motionless, except for the shy and tiny waves that the wind raises occasionally.
By the edge of the slope, several boulders have gathered; large, grey, rounded
boulders with smooth surfaces, cold as ice and hard as steel.
name: A Calm Reservoir

desc:
Despite the bleak look presented by the circular walls of this old building, and
although nothing but rubble remains of the ceiling, the heart of the mill
appears to be in good condition, as if the millstones were carved out of very
hard stone. Both stones take up most of the room. The lower millstone, bigger
and heavier, is placed below. Over it, the grindstone rotates when the mill is
in motion. A very worn wooden chute deposits the grain between them, and, once
milled, it can be collected in sacks through another hole.
name: Inside the Old Hobbit Mill

desc:
In this narrow cellar there is hardly any room to maneuver. A big waterwheel
occupies most of it; the structure and the paddles are made of wood, which, in
spite of their bad appearance don't seem shattered or broken. Two holes open
through the stone ceiling, leading to the upper floor. A tight, circular one,
right above the wheel; and another one similar to a trapdoor, but narrow and
uncomfortable-looking. The whole revolving mechanism is placed over some sort of
waterway, inclined along the hillside slope. The lack of space, and the fading
light that filters from above, may force you to grope your way along, to avoid
banging into something in the darkness.
name: The Wheel Room

desc:
A carpet of thick dust covers all the surfaces, as everywhere else in the hole,
but here it seems dotted with small black stains and filth of dubious source.
The walls show dark marks of damp, and so does the ceiling, flat near the door
but inclined in the rear end. A round dormer window leads outside there,
although the dense vegetation conceals it partially. A wide stain spreads
beneath it, exuding a piercing moisture smell. A broad bed, with legs worn away
long ago and rotten bedding, and an old wardrobe, full of holes like a sieve,
make up the furniture.
name: A Once Cosy Bedroom

desc:
Shadows now dwell in what once was a bustling living hall. Wrapped in a deep
darkness, an old hearth seems asleep, as if recalling ancient and innumerable
stories, songs, and gossip, that certainly filled this room when hobbits still
lived in Soakedfeet. One can still fancy the smell of the seed-cakes, or the
shrill wheeze of the teapot on the heavy oaken table that occupies the centre of
the room. The other furniture left with its owner, as well as the frills,
profuse judging by the numerous holes and stains on the walls, and with them
were gone the voices and tales, plunging the room into a deep silence.
name: A Dark and Silent Living Room

desc:
A one-finger-thick layer of grayish dust covers the ground of this small
entrance hall, crowding about the borders, right where the walls stand. In spite
of the prints of time, you can still distinguish their light yellow shade, and
of course the smooth round section, that melts with the ceiling. On the northern
wall, several marks reveal the positions that were once occupied by pictures,
and perhaps wall lights, and even a chest of drawers. Close to the door, a long
peg rack, darkened by the dust, still clings to the wall, and one can easily
picture a row of coloured hoods hanging from it. The well-excavated corridor
goes into the hill, but, to the south, it seems to open into a room.
name: A Dusty Entrance Hall

desc:
This short but carefully hollowed passage constitutes the hub of the hobbit
hole. Leading into the hill, a few rooms open along its sides, approaching the
hillsides in search of air and light. Its section is perfectly round, except, of
course, for the thoroughly even floor, now covered with a thick layer of dense,
grayish dust that gathers in piles, as if matted. The tunnel seems to come to an
end here, but, to the north, a wide hole leads into a shadowy room, while, on
the south, you can see a worn, green, oval-shaped door.
name: A Rounded Corridor

desc:
The very air of this tiny room seems old and heavy. It is rancid and thick after
years of stillness, and loaded with dust that scratches your throat and burns
your lungs as you breathe it. The room is in utter disorder: high stacks of
rusty tools and broken utensils, and heaps of old and useless junk intermingle
following no pattern. Agricultural implements, pots, and crockery all appear to
be in rather bad condition. Half-hidden among the rubbish at the foot of one of
the walls, a little hole, only a few inches wide, disappears into the rock, its
dark mouth wrapped in shadows.
name: A Small Closet

desc:
You are now in a small room with a lofted ceiling, that most probably follows
the surface of the hillside. Opposite the entrance, where the ceiling is lower
and more inclined, a half-rotten wooden shutter seems to have been closed for
centuries. Below it, the wall has been carved in the shape of a stone table,
with two holes for both a sink and a fireplace. The wall on the left seems
dotted in numerous rusty nails, from which one day surely hung all kind of
utensils and pots. On the contrary, a spacious larder opens to the right, inside
which the smell of food still appears to float in the air.
name: An Empty Kitchen

desc:
Many years ago, when the Third Age of Arda was still young, the first merry
hobbits with their bare feet and loudly-coloured clothes moved into these dales,
and they settled in this wide hill with gentle slopes, where the abundant grass
grows always high and wet, and thus they called it Soakedfeet. It has been a
long time since those little creatures migrated westwards beyond the Misty
Mountains, but the hill is still honeycombed with passageways, even though most
of the tunnels have collapsed and entrances are blocked. However, in the upper
part of the hill, near the top, a small, round door still seems to be in good
condition.
name: Soakedfeet Hill

desc:
A large, though rough, wooden building, undoubtedly a farm, stands to the north.
The surrounding fields have been cultivated and the ground divided into small
raked plots. A small trail starts right at the farm door, and leads southwards
zigzagging among the patches, seemingly bound for a small village with smoking
huts that lies in a wide valley between two low hillocks. Every now and then,
you hear a distant animal sound, but most of the times it is too faint to
identify it.
name: Cultivated Fields in Front of a Farm

desc:
High and splendid, the tender, juicy blades of grass are the best possible food
for the Beornings' animals. The soil must be extraordinarily fertile, for except
for some nibbled wisps, there are no clearings in the grasslands, as if every
night, new shoots of fresh grass would spring out to fill those areas where the
beasts graze during the day, thus the prairie always looking deep green and
radiant at each new dawn.
name: Fertile Pastures

desc:
You are now at the gentle slope of a broad hill covered in ever wet grass, its
flat, smooth-edged top slightly outstanding to the southwest. A tiny brook with
crystalline water comes down running through the fields from the south, and the
grass blades on its surface look like diminutive boats competing in a weird race
with no apparent goal, the sound of the flowing water encouraging them to rush.
Down the hillside, the small stream seems to fade away suddenly, as if swallowed
by the very earth.
name: A Tiny Stream through the Rich Grazing

desc:
A tiny stream, clear and limpid, springs suddenly out of a group of rocks, as
if, tired from being imprisoned in the underground, it wanted to feel the fresh
air of the vales again. Slithering along the surface of the stones, the drops
end up falling into a large flat rock, in an unceasing rattling, and forming a
reservoir in a hollow of the stone, whose walls, soft and smooth, have been
carved by the most skilled sculptor: Arda itself.
name: A Natural Watering Place

desc:
The grasslands roll out, covered with tall grass that dances and shifts in every
thinkable nuance of grey, green and yellow. As the grass moves, strange but
fascinating patterns are created, revealing sometimes the ground that is the
home of small animals that scurry around in their search for food. In all
directions the plains stretch out, as far as the eye can see.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The grass here is almost three feet high, but bends easily and does not seem to
hinder movement. The grass and several scattered bushes conceal the identities
of some small wild animals, undoubtedly on a quest for food. The grasslands
continue for almost as far as the eye can see.
name: Thriving Grassland

desc:
Grassland spans here as far as the eye can see. The ground is a mix of dark
green grass and hardy evergreen bushes. Far in the east, looms the shadow of a
dark forest. In the distance to the west, great peaks of a distant mountain
range can be seen.
name: Thriving Grassland

desc:
These fertile meadows are covered with tall, lush green grass that reaches a
man's waist. Small, beautiful flowers of all colours grow here. Even some
scattered bushes covered with flowers seem to have planted their roots here. To
the east the land is covered with grass, while to the west there lie more
meadowlands. Some trees grow tall and proud to the north.
name: Meadows

desc:
These beautiful and lush meadows are of a deep, emerald-green colour. The high
grass seems fresh where it stands in the fertile soil. Amidst the green, small
flowers add touches of red, blue and yellow to the picture, only making the
green of the grass appear even greener. In all directions but south the plains
stretch out.
name: Rolling Meadowlands

desc:
These vibrant meadows are the colour of emeralds, their lush, green grass
covering most of the ground. In the small spots where the grass does not grow,
beautiful flowers of red, blue and yellow grow in abundance. Surprisingly, the
fertile soil has not been cultivated, but to the south one can spot signs of
human hands: rows of beehives stand there. Some bees seem to have found their
way here in their search for new, fresh flowers. In all other directions the
plains stretch out.
name: Meadowlands

desc:
Here stand, in small groups, a variety of different trees. One can spot birches,
beeches and even a few lonely oaks. The grass that grows beneath the trees seems
to be shorter than that covering the plains, but not much else grows in the
shadow of the trees. To the south lie the more fertile vales of the Anduin,
while to the north, east and west the vast plains stretch out.
name: Forested Plains

desc:
Rising slowly over the plains, this soft slope ascends towards the top of a
small hillock to the east, where the old ruins of a wooden house still stand in
a rather precarious condition. If there ever was a track leading up the
hillside, it must have faded long ago, swallowed by the abundant grass that
carpets the ground in deep green. The rear wall of what seems to be a building
stands atop the hillock, a small door leading inside.
name: A Soft Slope to a Ruined Building

desc:
A rickety old large wooden house crowns the top of this low hillock. The walls,
made up of fat logs, are covered in thick moss and lichens, and exude a piercing
smell of moisture, but the fineness of the oaken wood has preserved them from
rotting. The roof seems to have borne worse fate - most of it caved in long ago
- and only the broadest balks stay at their place. In the front wall, the door
has tumbled down inwards, leaving a wide hole through which you can glimpse
numerous broken planks and debris. Over the lintel, a half-rotten wooden sign
seems about to fall off.
name: In Front of a Ruined Tavern

desc:
Over the gentle slope that ascends gradually southwards, the deep green of the
fresh grass mixes with the dark brown of the dug up earth, in small ploughed
patches with deep furrows. The tender shoots of some unrecognizable vegetables
spring up timidly out of the ground in some of them, but others already show
well-grown plants that conceal the dun earth beneath them. The soil is rich in
these lands, and the diverse crops feed their vigorous inhabitants throughout
the year.
name: A Cultivated Hillside

desc:
The tireless grasshoppers seem to have taken possession of this shallow, wet
depression of the ground. Time and time again, they climb up the grass blades to
jump from one shoot into another, often crashing in the air, but only to raise
themselves and start climbing again, in an endless cycle. Sometimes many of them
gather in a small area, and then the swarm appears as a dense mist that boils
with activity.
name: A Hollow in the Grasslands

desc:
In these lands, where water is abundant and grasslands extensive, the soil is
rich in life, and, from the Old Days onward, men have inhabited these dales. As
early as in the First Age, when the Great Enemy whose name shall not be
mentioned had not still been defeated, some tribes of men, that came from the
first Edain, settled in the northern vales. They were known as the Northmen,
although the elves called them Nenedain, and they were related to the men of
Rhovanion and to the ancestors of the Rohirrim. Nowadays, the main inhabitants
of these lands are the Beornings, who protect the vales against the orkish
threat from the mountains.
name: The Fertile Anduin Vales

desc:
Lots of ochre-coloured beehives line up on the flower-dotted grass, and they
impregnate the air with a strong sickly-sweet smell. The Beornings take care of
the bees, and plant flower patches for them. And in exchange, the bees supply
them with the honey they need to bake their honey pies, following the recipe
from their forebears. Some people even affirm that these true-hearted, rude men
can talk to their animals, by using a strange language based on sounds and
gestures, and, judging by their relationship, they may be right.
name: Rows of Beehives

desc:
A small field of green grass, tall and dense, spreads out here. A coarse fence,
made out of unequal oaken branches covered in moss, surrounds its borders, but
not totally enclosing it. The Beornings look after and feed their animals, which
in turn obey them of their own free will. In the western side, a rickety door
leads into a thatched-roofed shed, from where you occasionally hear a loud
neigh. Right behind it stands a larger wooden building, surrounded by cultivated
fields; most likely a farm.
name: A Fenced Field

desc:
Several insects, almost too small to be seen, fly between the blades of grass
here. The dark green grass must contain some nutrients they crave. They leave no
mark on the vegetation and seem to cause no problem for travellers or the small
animals that can be heard to the north. Far off to the west, the high peaks of a
mountain range scar the horizon. A good distance to the east, the tops of many
tall trees mark the beginning of a great forest.
name: Thriving Grassland

desc:
Three foot tall grass grows through a mass of brown shrubbery. Some smaller
bushes try to carve out a niche for themselves to grow in, but appear to be
losing ground. Tracks of small animals, perhaps scampering for food, are
abundant.
name: High Grasses

desc:
The flat grasslands are fairly featureless. The thick green grass grows in
abundance. The ground is neither rocky nor rolling, instead it merely sits,
holding the weight of things placed on top of it. Some animals can be heard and
the occasional gust of wind rustles the blades of grass.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The ground is a mix of dark-green grass and shrubs. Some small animals leave
tracks in the ground as they go on with their daily routine of searching for
food and shelter among the vegetation. In the northwest, a small hut can be
seen. Far in the east lies a forest.
name: Vibrant Grasslands

desc:
Everything seems mixed up and out of its place in this small, low-roofed
kitchen. The sink is full of crockery, while the pantry is almost empty, the
food scattered all over the most unlikely places. There are sacks lying on the
ground, and jars standing on the table, together with full and empty pots and
even an apron hanging from the back of a chair; all is in utter disorder.
Several escape routes, rather than exits, flee out from this mess. To the south,
a broad wooden door leads outside; to the east, a smaller one conducts into a
small shed; and a winding staircase, with high, narrow steps, ascends into the
upper floor through a trapdoor hole.
name: An Untidy Kitchen

desc:
This narrow and long room faces east, its axis following the line of the roof.
Floor, walls and ceiling are all made of well polished, creaking, wooden boards,
that shelter the inside from the winter cold, the persistent damp, and the
annoying trickles. On the eastern wall, a round window lights the room. At dawn,
it casts the light directly upon the bed, announcing the start of a new working
day. A few planks, a mattress that is basically a heap of dry straw, and some
thick wool blankets make up the double bed a tiny bedside table stands near its
head. At the foot of the bed, beside the trapdoor hole, an old chest completes
the furniture.
name: A Simple Bedroom

desc:
Without other purpose than giving the animals shelter and cover during the
night, this shed isn't even distributed in sections. On the contrary, the
different heaps of dry straw delimit the room, and the animals occupy their own
without any trouble. By the rear wall, there are piles of still fresh and green
hay, and a great stack of straw crowned by a large hoe. Several implements lean
against the walls in one of the corners. Everything is somewhat in disorder, but
clean and rather free of insects. A small door on the west links the shed to a
large house in front of it, a waist-high gate leads into a meadow to the east.
name: A Small Side Shed

desc:
The grasslands abound in all directions. Some miles to the east some dark trees
turn into a forest. The animal life seems prevalent by the scraps of food and
the scarring of claws and teeth marks in the vegetation. On the western horizon,
travellers can stare in awe at the snowy peaks of the great mountains.
name: Vibrant Grasslands

desc:
Thick grass grows in the shade of the tall young trees. Much of the light that
would hit this area in the morning, is blotted out by the thick dark forest to
the east. Only in the afternoon do the plants get the sunlight they need to
live.
name: Grasslands by Mirkwood Forest

desc:
This is the very edge of the eastern side of the Mirkwood Forest. Tall, old
trees with dark leaves and black bark cast a gloomy shadow. A few small bushes
try to carve out their place in the shaded ground. The view into the forest
offers no clue to its contents and shows no sign of a clearing. To the west are
the plains consisting of miles of flat land, a welcome change from Mirkwood.
name: Border of Mirkwood

desc:
Gloom fills the air, as the lack of light is discouraging. Visibility is poor
and the trees all look very similar. They have bark so black it blends into the
night and reflect any stream of light in the day. Rustling in the top branches
can be heard. Who knows what creatures would find this dreary a setting a
suitable habitat.
name: Inside Mirkwood

desc:
A small break in the top branches lets a ray of light fall down illuminating the
surroundings. The light reveals the forest to be even gloomier lit than when
dark. The dirt is teeming with tiny bugs and some smelly unrecognisable
bacteria. The remains of what was once a small squirrel is here decaying.
Perhaps it is better that most of the forest is not well lit.
name: Inside Mirkwood Forest

desc:
How the dense plants that make up the undergrowth survive without light is a
mystery. They grow underneath and between the fallen limbs of the trees. A thin
vine grows between two of the trees trying to reach beyond the tops in search of
a bit of sunlight. The forest looks the same in every direction.
name: Inside Mirkwood

desc:
One of the great trees of Mirkwood is here, lifeless. Obstructed by the other
trees, it was unable to fall completely to the ground. What felled this
monstrosity with it's 30 foot trunk, is a mystery but it must have happened long
ago. Dead leaves still cling to its branches though they crumble to the touch.
name: At a Fallen Tree

desc:
Whether it is the sun or the moon that shines above the tree-formed canopy is
unknown. What little gleam of light enters here reflects on torn pieces of
spider silk from what seems to have been a great web. The spider silk is old and
no longer sticky to the touch. Instead it looks like thin hairs strewn
carelessly over the dark branches.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Even though not far from the forest's border, there is no sign of a break in the
trees in any direction. Little light reaches this place. The sound of scurrying
animals above in the treetops alerts travellers they are not alone.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
During the day the small amount of light that reaches here makes the black sap
leaking from some of the trees looks gelatinous. Small insects have been trapped
and killed just by landing in a sticky spot. The leaking sap has also captured
some small black pieces of fur.
name: Very Thick Forest

desc:
It's dark as dusk here even at noon. In a constant struggle for light it is
amazing that any plant life on the ground survives. A few sturdy bushes have
managed to grow and some shade grasses send straggle blades up through the mess
of fallen branches on the ground.
name: Inside Mirkwood Forest

desc:
A canopy of high tree limbs overhead signals that you are within the Mirkwood
forest. The ancient dark trees are thick, but here at the edge of the forest
they still let some light pass through to the undergrowth. There is almost no
sign of animal life, though perhaps there are a few rabbits and squirrels
nearby.
name: Stepping into the Mirkwood Forest

desc:
Some young trees grow here just outside the forest to the south. They are a
welcome change in the plant life from the deep dark gloom of Mirkwood. Their
light brown bark has a sweet smell of sap. So pleasant are these trees that many
birds have left nests within the branches.
name: Forested Plains

desc:
This forest fairway stretching from north-south is home to many woodland
flowers. Purple blooms of delicate columbine tower above the multi-hued green
mosses covering the soil here. Blue lupine and pale yellow daisies with brown
eyes peek above the shorter grasses.
name: Forest Fairway

desc:
These shadowy plains only receive a short supply of sun as they are bordered on
both sides by the gloomy Mirkwood forest. The woods have grown in such a dense
formation that no entryway is found easily. Along the edges of the forest new
saplings crowd competing for space. The fields continue both north and south.
name: The Narrows

desc:
The trees block out all the sun until late into the afternoon. There are no
signs of animals here, just a few insects flying from leaf to leaf. One suspects
the small plains animals do not venture into the woods and those animals native
to Mirkwood Forest dare not show themselves this far west. The smell of rotten
wood is in the air.
name: Border of Mirkwood

desc:
Interwoven groups of tall branches form a canopy blocking out the sky. It
extends far to the east. Those travelling into the forest better get a good look
at the sky while they can. Already the trees block out all the morning sun. Even
at high noon not much light touches the ground.
name: Border of Mirkwood

desc:
The vegetation is much thicker here than in the west. Rotten branches lay on the
ground, catching and tearing at the skin of animals and travellers. Under the
protection of these branches some small animals seem to move shielded from view.
A careful listener can hear the sounds of dark squirrels moving in the live
branches up above.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The forest is dark and hides all but the eyes of some small dark creatures
living high within the branches of the trees. They don't seem to be aggressive
but may distract travellers from more eminent dangers here. A sticky film, or
perhaps just the leak of sap, almost black in colour, causes dead leaves to
stick to the large trunks.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The leaves of the trees are very thick, and dark green in colour. The small
amount of light that reaches the ground, must first pass through the thick
leaves of the trees, then slip between the gaps in the crooked branches and
hanging vines. The large bushes that grow here must thrive in shade. There are a
lot of rotting branches and leaves lying on the ground.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
This is the very edge of the Mirkwood Forest. It lies just a few yards to the
east. The trees within its boundaries are dark and foreboding. Almost no sun
reaches the ground, blocked by a canopy of branches. The ground is covered with
rotten branches and leaves.
name: Stepping into Mirkwood Forest

desc:
The plains in the west are slowly turning into the forest in the east. Some
trees can be seen here and there, and bushes have become more common. Far in the
west, the snow-capped Misty Mountains can be seen. To the east nothing can be
seen but the gloominess of the edge of the Mirkwood Forest.
name: Forested Plains

desc:
Small animals too quick to be seen dart between the trees and bushes. From the
scratches in the ground and the bite marks in some half eaten seeds, one could
assume these are the same type of moles that roam the western plains, although
few dare travel this far east.
name: Plains by Mirkwood

desc:
Rich grains can be found in abundant supply here. The countryside sweeps gently
in easy rises and rolls of the grassy terrain. The seasons have favoured this
area and the rich bounty is appreciated by the wildlife. The tight formation of
the grove of trees to the north does not offer any entry in that direction.
name: Great Meadow

desc:
Many of the tree-trunks here have been severely damaged. It looks like the bark
has been deeply scratched leaving big gaping grooves in the trunks. This damage
looks as if it has been done quite recently as fresh sticky resin is oozing from
the wounds. To the east and south the vegetation is so thick that it is
impossible to travel far in those directions.
name: Corner of the Forest

desc:
Oaks and alder are common in this forest. It does not take long to notice the
magnificent oaks with dense foliage towering majestically above. In the
immediate area nothing else grows, aside from the fungi and mosses that grow
rapidly. To the west and south the forest is so thick that there is no possible
way to travel in either of those directions.
name: Forest Corner

desc:
These fields are overgrown with thatch. Grains grow in abundance, despite the
assault of this fast growing pesky weed, which has travelled every which way.
This terrain makes travel through these parts difficult and to the north the
dense grove of trees prevents travel in that direction. Several large stones
terrace the western corner.
name: Wild Pasture

desc:
A tree to the west, about a mile away, is the most noticeable feature here. The
plains are otherwise flat and free from tall growth. The air has a calm serenity
and it carries the smell of hay. These grasses have grown here for ages, green
through the summers and brown in winter. In the east lies the great dark forest
of Mirkwood.
name: Plains

desc:
Seeds blown from nearby trees have taken root here. The barely two foot tall
tree growths bend and break easily under the hoofs of animals. Dark green grass
covers the land far to the north and west. These little trees might be a sign of
the forest that is visible to the east.
name: Plains

desc:
The plains here are dotted with young trees, spread from a nearby forest. The
trees seem strong and sturdy. Their deep brown bark seems to resist the cut of
animal claws or the whip of the wind. Gazing to the east you see more trees.
They seem to be a great deal more seasoned.
name: Forested Plains

desc:
This is the very edge of the legendary Mirkwood forest. If there is a pathway
through here it's not discernible. The trees grow tall and the lower plants
sprawl through their roots without limits. Some almost invisible insects fly
about annoying those trying to peer into the forest's depths.
name: Edge of the Dark Forest

desc:
This is the edge of Mirkwood. Its trees, hundreds of years old, tower above the
ground. What one can only assume is rich soil is covered with rotting branches
and cumbersome tangles of low vines. Looking east it is impossible to see
anything but more of these ancient trees.
name: Edge of the Dark Forest

desc:
The ground is covered with large dark-green bushes, many old and tall trees grow
here. The vegetation is mostly coloured dark green, but the bark of the trees is
dark brown. In the east, the forest gets even thicker, while in the west,
grasslands grow ever thicker.
name: Edge of the Dark Forest

desc:
This is the very edge of the huge Mirkwood forest. Large trees, hundreds of
years old grow here, towering above some large thick bushes. The vegetation is
mostly dark-green. Nothing beyond the forest can be seen in the east, while in
the west, endless grasslands lie for miles.
name: Edge of the Dark Forest

desc:
The ground is covered with large dark-green bushes, and old and tall trees grow
here. The vegetation is mostly coloured dark green, but the bark of the trees is
dark brown. In the east, the forest gets even thicker, while in the west,
grasslands take over.
name: Grasslands by Dense Forest

desc:
Tall young trees grow here above a carpet of thick green grass. A few plants
grow in the shade of the trees. This area gets little morning light, the
sunlight blocked by the large dark forest to the east. The tracks of some small
animals are scratched in the ground. On a hot day the shade of the trees and
then softness of the grass beckons many a traveller to rest here.
name: Grasslands by Mirkwood

desc:
The ground is covered with three-feet high dark-green grass. Scattered bushes
stake claim to the ground where the grass has not spread. Wild animals, moles
and others, have marked the vegetation with their teeth and claws. To the east,
a dark forest is a far contrast to these flatlands. Far in the west, a mountain
range covers much of the horizon.
name: High Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands continue westward for miles, but in the east clumps of trees turn
into a forest. The empty holes left behind by moles ensnare the feet of careless
travellers. The holes are hard to see through the three foot thick grass. A far
distance to the west the peaks of the mountains are visible.
name: Untended Grasslands

desc:
There are several holes dug in the ground here, underneath the three-foot high
grass. The grasslands go on for miles to the west. In the east scattered trees
turn into a forest. The peaks of the great Misty Mountains are visible on the
western horizon.
name: Grasslands by Mirkwood

desc:
Grasslands give way to trees as the lands turns into a forest. Bushes thrive in
the tree shaded shadows. The ground is a haven for vine-like plants that form a
thick undergrowth. It is impossible to see too far into the dark forest to the
east.
name: Grassy Plains by Mirkwood

desc:
The low grass filling the western plains gradually give way to this land of
larger bushes and trees. The trees have light brown bark, but to the east the
trees are darker. From here, the trees in the forest, just two or three miles
away, look black as night.
name: Plains by the Forest

desc:
The plains in the west are slowly turning into forest. To the east the trees
blot out light. The young trees here easily leave enough light to nurture
undergrowth. There are few animal markings perhaps they fear the proximity of
this area to the dark forest.
name: Plains by the Forest

desc:
The grass here is thin. Weeds grow quickly covering the bare space on the
ground. The land is flat and featureless. Travellers find it is easily passable
but the monotony of the area disheartens and visitors tend to meander. The
remains of a past campsite, a stone circle where a fire must have burned long
ago, almost encourages current travellers to rest here.
name: Overgrown Campsite

desc:
The sprouts of small trees poke out above the grass. Perhaps the seeds from
nearby trees blew here with the wind. They are rooted among the grass and only
time will tell if they survive to grow into trees. If left untouched they might
have grown high into the clouds.
name: Plains by Some Trees

desc:
A lone tree stands here in the middle of the plains. Perhaps a seed from the
group of trees to the west once drifted here. The tree casts a long shadow on
the low grass below. A few small plants try to take advantage of the shade.
name: Plains

desc:
Many different grains germinate freely here in abundance in all areas but one.
There is a small hump in the field to the southeast. Around that swelling the
grasses are meagre and matted down, well worn with activity and use.
name: Mixed Fields

desc:
The meadows meet impassable forest walls of the Mirkwood here to the north and
east. The tall woods shelter this area allowing dense foliage to prosper. The
few trees dotting the fields have fallen victim to vines which hang from every
limb. Flowering azalea and camellia reside over the ferns in the remainder of
this forest dell.
name: Corner Fields

desc:
This area is covered with debris from seasons of dead foliage. It is overgrown
with vines, creating a dense thicket. Bright purple blooms of thistles on pale
prickly stems shoot up between the foliage of untamed, creeping, thorny vines.
There are many large bushes, as tall as any man, with healthy green leaves above
spent barren limbs.
name: Thicket

desc:
Several trees here grow close together. Their roots intertwine far below the
surface. Above the ground, weeds have grown wildly into the size of small
bushes. Marks on the tree trunks and damage to the flora give hint of a large
animal or animals nearby. The thick vegetation tends to obstruct your view.
name: A Small Group of Trees

desc:
The trees are denser here than to the north or west. Wild plants are plentiful
within their shaded shelter. On the ground are the tracks of animals, some
predators others prey. Low branches and tall plants have been trampled or broken
forming animal paths.
name: A Small Group of Trees

desc:
The clear views towards the horizon, becomes a thing of the past here. Trees
close by begin to blot out the view. It is difficult to see much else than their
many outstretched branches. Broken bones of a small mole are on the ground. The
bones are badly decayed and would crumble to the touch. Whether this animal died
of natural causes or was broken in two by the jaws of a larger beast is unknown.
name: Plains by Some Trees

desc:
Bushes conceal small animals frightened by the presence of strangers. The grass
is thinner here, it's roots not as strongly knit together. A few strands of
onion grass poke out from the lower grass.
name: Plains

desc:
The grass that grows here is coloured dark green, and some small bushes are
growing about. The animals in the undergrowth are going on with their daily
routine for food. In the east, the plains start turning into a light forest, but
in the south and west, grasslands seem to take over.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
The ground and vegetation is slowly changing. To the south, the grass grows
higher and becomes thicker. To the north, the grass thins and does not rise far
off the ground. The white caps of the Misty Mountains can be seen far to the
west.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Thick green grass covers the ground here. Some of the taller blades show the
signs of being trampled upon in the not too distant past. Perhaps by travellers
on horseback or the carefree roaming of some animals. Toward the south the grass
look like it may have once been under the care of some diligent gardener.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass, while mostly wild, seems to have spread here from what may once have
been a well-tended lawn. There are few bare spots and the grass seems to all be
of a similar variety. Only a few bushes and the grass is only broken up by the
recent growth of weeds.
name: Untended Grasslands

desc:
To the west is the front door of a small wooden building. An old path leads from
an in-ground well to the door. From here you can see storm damage to the
structure's roof. Whole pieces of roof are missing, presumably fallen into the
house. Some neatly chopped wood rots south of the entrance.
name: Grasslands before a Small Hut

desc:
Wild weeds invade this area. They leave little room for bushes or even a small
tree to take root. The ground was once tilled and the remnants of what looks
like a vegetable garden remain. Some ignored potatoes have sprouted into plants.
There is a small wooden house to the east.
name: Behind the Hut

desc:
The grass begins to thin a little, and various weeds have taken root. Rotting
plants lay upended about the area, their sweet stench filling the air. Dirt has
been thrown carelessly around, in no particular pattern.
name: Sparser Grass

desc:
The vast plains and seamless grasslands end here giving way to more varied
vegetation. Weeds that flower and less sturdy plants grow harmoniously in this
dark soil. What enables this land to host this wide variety of life is yet to be
seen. To the west a great mountain range looms on the horizon.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The northern plains become southern grasslands here. This in-between area has
grasses of varying lengths and some sporadic shrubbery. A few moles live here
but not as many as in other parts of the region. Other small grazing animals
have left their hoof marks in the ground.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
To the south of this spot the plains gradually become grassier. One might
suspect a change in the soil for the change to a hardier vegetation and taller
grass. Some mole holes can be seen in the ground, but the moles keep to the
underground.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
There is little break in the thick grass of these plains. This dark grass is so
tightly rooted together that it would be next to impossible for any other
vegetation to grow through. The odd sound of a mole tunnelling in the earth
nearby seems unusually loud since it is the only thing audible.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
A few leaves from the trees to the north lay in the grass. Some carried by the
wind others by the scurrying of animals. The trees offer a pleasant distraction
to the monotony of dark green grass that carpets most of the plains. Some small
flying bugs almost invisible to the naked eye jump from one blade of grass to
another.
name: Verdant Plains

desc:
A group of tall trees grow here breaking up the monotony of the plains, here
with bark so dark it's almost black. These trees have not grown to even half
their potential yet. Some squirrels have left scratches in the trunk and
branches. Nuts, cracked open and those just lying on the ground appear to have
little hope of taking seed.
name: A Small Group of Trees

desc:
This group of trees is a welcome contrast to the barrenness of the plains. These
are middle-aged trees, stretching tall into the sky, but not so old as to cast a
large shadow upon the land. Some bushes grow in the small amount of shade
provided. An almost dead smell emanates from the trees that are most assuredly
alive. The footprints of some large animals are embedded in the dirt.
name: A Small Group of Trees

desc:
Seedy grasses fill these fields with waist-high stems. Spurge blankets the rich
soil below. The ideal growing conditions in these pastures yield enough grain to
satisfy the wildlife here. There are trees north and south with the plains
continuing to the east and west.
name: Everplains

desc:
A tight circle of chestnut trees stands together in these grassy wheat fields.
Spent shells litter the ground below them. Mustard and fennel season this broad
field with their presence. Far to the east the dark forest grows.
name: Ring of Chestnuts

desc:
There is as much exposed bedrock as dirt in these fields of scrub grasses.
Silver grey foliage of tough rock garden creeper circles the exposed stone.
Bristles of dry stickers stand above the meagre crabgrass and weeds in these
rough grounds. The plains continue to the north and south, while the hostile
terrain seems to carry on west.
name: Scrubby Fields

desc:
Plain bent grass seems to dominate the other plants in these fields, growing low
and stealthily across the plains. Tiny burrs covered in thin hairy barbs hide
among these grasses awaiting passers-by to snag. Lesser grasses with thin
delicate green stems are topped by lacy seed heads of pale gold.
name: Low-lying Meadow

desc:
The hustle and bustle of a variety of small animal life dominates this area.
Moles and other small animal scurry about their daily routine just out of sight.
To the east is a small group of trees. In all other directions, the plains
stretch onward for miles.
name: Plains by Some Trees

desc:
The grass here grows higher than the surrounding plains. Perhaps something in
the soil is causing it to thrive. What ever the cause, the tall grass has a
strong sweet smell that lingers in the air. To the east are some trees. Far to
the west, the caps of a mountain range loom in the distance.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
Animal tracks have worn down the thick green grass in several spots. The few
bushes have been scoured for berries and small insects. These plains must be
teeming with small animals. Yet, none can be seen. The plains continue in all
directions for miles.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
The tall tops of onion grass rise above the dark green grass that covers most of
this area. The bite marks of small animals into the onion greenery has let lose
the sweet onion scent. A few other grass varieties also grow here. To support so
much growth the soil must be rich in nutrients not found in other parts of these
plains.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
The smell of wild onion overwhelms here bringing tears to the eyes of many a
passer by. The grass grows meagrely in comparison to the thriving onions.
Several small animals have left their teeth prints in these plants. Perhaps they
were drawn to the rich smell and then surprised by the bitter taste.
name: Wild Onion Plains

desc:
The grass here is shorter than to the north or south. It is dark green in colour
and the thick blades of grass cling together. A few wild onions have taken seed
here. The plains to the north seem overrunning with strands of tall onion grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Dark green grass, almost three feet high, covers the ground. Some moles have dug
holes here, but rarely do they dare show themselves. In the north the grass is
lower, perhaps due to changes in the soil. Far to the east lies a dark forest.
In the distant west is a mountain range.
name: Untamed Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands stretch out for miles in all directions. The grass is dark green,
and almost three feet high. Some small wild animals are searching for food among
the few small bushes that grow nearby.
name: Verdant Grasslands

desc:
The scattered remains of nuts and berries lay here. Some small animal, probably
a mole, appears to have had a feast. There are several dirt strewn mole holes
dug into the grass. The thick grass is a dark green and thrives on the soil in
these parts, spreading out in all directions.
name: Grasslands

desc:
Animals scurry about faster than the time needed to make out their species. They
hide within the dark green grass like thieves gathering berries and seeds before
larger animals might have the opportunity to feed on them. The few bushes that
grow here have been damaged by scratches and bite marks. To the north a few
mole-holes can be seen.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The vegetation nestled within the high grass here, has been scavenged for every
last berry and seed. The small animals here devour the plant life in staggering
amounts. The competition between moles, hiding deep in their holes must be
fierce.
name: Mole infested Grasslands

desc:
The odd wild plant breaks up the thick carpet of dark green grass. Sounds from
below ground indicate that a mole colony is busy at work. The grass stretches
for almost as far as the eye can see. The land is flat and monotonous.
Travellers may find this area quite unremarkable, however the small animals
thrive in the consistency of this easily passable land.
name: Untamed Grasslands

desc:
This is the home of several small animals. Moles have dug their homes down into
the soil. The grass is dark green and covers much of the area. A careful
observer would notice small bite marks in the blades of grass and the lower
limbs of the few bushes. The rustling within the higher grass can only be the
scurrying of the small mammals in their search for food.
name: Untamed Grasslands

desc:
Some small round rocks are scattered randomly between the blades of grass.
Though the soil here supports hardy dark green grass, the rocks indicate that
this land was once barren. Few bushes or shrubs can survive here. The land is
flat for miles and there is no shelter from the wind.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
Thick blades of grass grow through the rocky soil. Their roots not well embedded
in the ground give hint that this land was once barren. Very little other than
the grass will even grow. Though the flat land is home to several sturdy shrubs
and a few bushes.
name: Plains

desc:
A large boulder sits here, breaking the monotony of the surrounding plains. It
is the only noticeable landmark in the bleak and dreary land. Several small
bushes have taken refuge on the lee side of the massive rock. The soil here is
dusty and inhospitable to little more than the dark green grass that dominates
much of these plains.
name: At a Large Boulder

desc:
The plains here continue on to the horizon in all directions. Caution is needed
to avoid stepping in one of the many mole-holes dug into this land. Grasses in
many shades, from dark to pale green, grow in clumps, perhaps concealing the
moles who make this land their home.
name: Lively Plains

desc:
The flat lay of this land allows for a great deal of visibility. The dark grass
continues for some distance without break of hill or valley. In the distance
appears to be the mountains. The taller peaks almost seem to meet the clouds.
Dense trees far to the east form a forest.
name: Lush Plains

desc:
Dull bent grass and weedy spurge cover these grounds. Several evergreen shrubs
form a low bushy mound to the south. The plains continue on in every direction.
A smattering of small hills indicates some rodent population uprising.
name: Bent Sod

desc:
A butte of seemingly solid grey-blue granite stands as tall as a Hobbit's knee
The deep cracks along the rock protects the multitudes of insects that live
within them. A deep vale to the north prevents travel in that direction.
Voracious creeping vines find ideal conditions here as their foliage clings
securely to the textured rock face.
name: Rocky Rise

desc:
A low rock outcropping is jutting out from the ground encircled by alder trees.
Rambling wild roses favour this area and bloom in hues of red and coral
throughout the year. The stony crag creates a good cover behind the trees. The
ravine to the north prevents travel in that direction.
name: Stony Outcropping

desc:
Here the fields offer only meagre swatches of oat grass and straggly clumps of
fennel. Except for the efforts of these grasses and some alder saplings, the
wide expanses of stony earth leave this plain hopelessly barren. The plains
continue to the south and to the west.
name: Stone Studded Terrace

desc:
The ground is fairly flat and featureless. Different shades of green vegetation
provides some contrast. Small bushes and shrubs stick out among the lower plant
life. Small footprints give evidence of some animals' search for food. A quick
glance around shows the neighbouring land to be similarly inhabited.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
Dark green grass lies here between the occasional lighter coloured plant. The
ground is home to some sturdy shrubs and some hearty bushes. The sound of
trickling water comes from the south. Looking toward the east a dark forest
appears over the horizon.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
A small spring of clear water gently flows through the surrounding grasses. At
several places the ground is water-logged, while other spots are merely moist.
The land slopes upward to the north and east, leading to some drier land. To the
south and west the land retains more moisture.
name: A Small Spring

desc:
Water seeping into this land from a nearby spring has made this land moist.
Heather thrive here nestled among other wetland plants. Shrubbery contributes to
the marshiness of this flat area. The sound of water trickling can be heard to
the north.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
A light gurgling sound can be heard beneath the damp soil. These water-soaked
plains are likely fed by an underground river. The vegetation is thicker here
and heather grows in abundance. The ground is soft, and somewhat slick.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
The grasslands here are moist, hinting that a river may not be far away. A small
variety of bushes grow here, but most common is the sturdy heather. Shapes on
the western horizon seem to resemble a mountain range. Toward the east, the
grasslands seem to give way to a forest.
name: Moist Grasslands

desc:
The grass here is hardy from years of soaking up water and nutrients from this
moist soil. It forms a soft carpet of green blades that bend to pressure and
quickly rise up again with the blow of a gentle wind. While there are no small
animals to be seen, the sense that this might be grazing land is a strong one.
name: Moist Grasslands

desc:
Grass grows together in tight clumps so thick that it hides the richness of the
soil. The hardy blades seem fit enough to withstand wind and the change of
seasons. Even the disruption of heavy footsteps would not do much damage to this
carpet of green.
name: Thriving Grasslands

desc:
The deep green grass seems to thrive here leaving little room for larger plants
to take seed and grow. Spanning miles northward and just as far to the south,
the grass seemingly goes on forever. The wide variety of tracks attest that a
healthy amount of traffic comes through here, however the flora remains unharmed
by animals or mounted travellers.
name: Heart of the Grasslands

desc:
Thick grass cushions a traveller's step here. The grass seems to thrive from
some source of abundant water nearby. The blades of grass are resilient; when
bent by winds or stepped upon they quickly regain their skyward climb. To the
south the grass flourishes, growing noticeably higher.
name: Abundant Grasslands

desc:
Blades of grass shoot up through the moist soil. To the south and to the east
the grass seems to be densely packed. Northward the ground is more saturated
with water than it is here. The grass bends to the will of the wind as it gently
sweeps through. Crackling sounds can be heard in the bushes.
name: Moist Grasslands

desc:
The ground here is teeming with moisture, and it sinks down under any weight.
Several shrubs that crave moisture thrive while plant life that requires a more
balanced environment cannot survive the overabundance of water. Some of the less
prickly shrubs seem to have been grazed upon by small animals.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
Scattered bushes seem to thrive in the water rich soil. The plant life here
consists of sturdy heather and some water grasses. Footprints are abundant, as
anything that weighs down on this land sinks into the moist topsoil.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
Water fills the plains here, the source of which is unknown. Perhaps it is an
underground river but no sign of this is apparent. Wild animal tracks are on the
ground between some scattered bushes. The plains become a dark forest in the
distance to the east. Far on the western horizon lies a mountain range.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
This expanse of land is extremely flat. The ground is saturated with muddy
groundwater, and only light patches of bushes exist. Heather is more plentiful
here and are by far the dominant plant species. On the far reaching western
horizon an emerging mountain range can be seen. To the east the ground rises
slightly, and far away a dark green forest takes shape.
name: Wet Plains

desc:
The ground here is soft and moist. Water is poised to fill sunken depressions in
the soil. The vegetation here is less dense than the surrounding areas. In
places nothing seems to grow through what appears to be mud. There are no trees
nor bushes, yet some heather appears to be sufficiently maturing.
name: Moist Plains

desc:
These plains appear to be endless. They continue in every direction as far as
the naked eye can see. To the south, however, the land seems a bit wetter. Often
one can watch the blades of grass gently swaying with the forces of passing
winds. Some small animals rustle about in the undergrowth.
name: Endless Plains

desc:
Amongst the dark green grass scattered bushes grow, rounding out the vast
landscape. Mole-holes dot the area and one can often catch a glimpse of the
small animals as they scurry about for food. Far to the west, the cloud covered
caps of a mountain range are visible. A distance to the east, a dark forest
looms.
name: Endless Plains

desc:
Bushy plains of tall mounding grasses with surrounds of exposed topsoil. The
lush fields break up here in all directions. West of here the fields grow high
and dense, while to the east the grasses grow sparse. Several showy butterfly
bushes have taken root here.
name: Changing Plains

desc:
Lush fields of ryegrass, fescue and wheat grass surround this area. To the north
a precariously steep ravine cuts across the meadow from east to west, preventing
travel in that direction. The edges of the narrow gorge are rimmed in sharp,
thorny bramble preventing anything from wandering too close to the drop.
Handsome foliage of day lilies stands out against the wild grasses.
name: Central Pastures

desc:
This grass looks more like reed: the blades are thick and knotty with long
narrow leaves. The view is almost blocked by the high foliage, but the plains
seem to continue in all directions. Indeed the vegetation must be a good shelter
for animals. The grass has grown shorter to the east but still reaches far
enough to cover possible shrubs.
name: Man-high Grass

desc:
Tall tough foliage covers the area as far as eyes can reach. The shorter
travellers and inhabitants have to jump up or climb on each other to reach above
the grass. Traces of many animals can be spotted around, as anything that moves
has to force its way through the plants. The grass grows shorter both to the
south and to the east.
name: Man-high Grass

desc:
The plains here are covered with low grass in all shades of green, yellow and
grey. To the west low hills obstruct the view slightly, but in all other
directions the view of the surrounding lands that stretch out gently beneath the
great skies is unobstructed. To the south stands a lonely tree that seems to
support the sky above you with its branches.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Here, alone on the plains, stands a lonely tree. What kind of tree it is seems
hard to tell, but whatever it may be, it must be a hard wood that can so defy
the winds that constantly buffet these plains. It appears like a pillar of the
sky, supporting the great vault of the heavens with its thick branches.
name: A Large Tree in the Plains

desc:
These grasslands seem to be almost marshy, as they lie in a small dip in the
plains. The ground is soft and moist with water that seems to be gushing up from
some hidden underground source below. The grass grows in tufts, searching for
drier place to take root. The stems that remain in the mucky soil grow yellow
and soaked. To the south, the wet land continues, while in other directions the
land seems to dry.
name: Moist Grasslands

desc:
Here the grasslands are turning marshy. Soggy footing makes the traveller search
for drier spots, to avoid getting stuck in the mash of mixed plants and mud.
Brown water trickles up from under ground, its trace yet unseen. To the north
the moist plains continue, while in other directions the land seems to dry up.
name: Wet Grasslands

desc:
The grasslands roll out under the great skies above, forever shifting and
dancing playfully with the wind. As the grass moves, it also shifts in colour,
being of all thinkable nuances of green, yellow and grey. In all directions the
plain stretches out, but to the north the ground seems to be marshy.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The grass shifts and dances playfully as the wind rushes in from the north and
west. Rustling and singing with the wind, the grass creates strange but
beautiful patterns in the landscape that change constantly. In all directions
the plain stretches out under the great skies overhead.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The grass here seems higher and thicker than that which can be found on the
plains surrounding it. It stands almost as tall as a man, the blades shifting
and rustling gently in the wind. One can presume that this would be the perfect
place for a predator of the plains to hide in, stalking its victim silently
through the dense grass.
name: High Grass

desc:
The grass rolls out endlessly beneath the great vault of the sky above,
evocating an image of emptiness. The fresh winds that come tumbling in from the
northwest carry with them the scent of fresh herbs and grass. Into the east the
ground grows moist underfoot, while to the west some bushes can be spotted in
the plains.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
These empty plains lie like a great green carpet beneath the giant sky, that
stretched from the one end of the horizon to another. Sounds of small birds and
animals are mixed in with the constant whispering of the wind, creating a low
drone, buzzing in the ears of the passerby. In all directions, the plains
stretch out.
name: Plains

desc:
These gently rolling grasslands have been blessed with large and healthy beeches
that stand tall and proud. Their straight trunks extending high into the sky
that looms above. Northwards the grasslands become covered with bushes, while to
the south a fertile meadow catches the eye.
name: Sparsely Wooded Grasslands

desc:
These grasslands are covered with all kinds of bushes, from low and thorny
shrubs to larger bushes covered with tiny violet blooms. Between the bushes,
small flowers and an occasional orchid seem to have found shelter from the wind.
In all directions the plains stretch out under the great dome of the sky.
name: Bushy Grasslands

desc:
These grasslands are covered with the tall, flowing grass of deep-green colour
that is familiar for the fields around this landscape. No tree or even a small
bush obstructs the view of the plains. To the south, the land becomes bushy,
giving shelter for small flowers that dot the grass. In all other directions,
the lush fields continue.
name: Grasslands

desc:
The grassy plains roll out beneath the giant sky above in a never-ending dance
with the land. To the west lies the only height visible: a small but rather high
grassy mound that stands out sharply against the sky. Between the grass small
patches of dirt are exposed, looking like some creature has just dug here.
Animal droppings confirm the existence of some kind of wildlife. Higher
grassland can be seen to the south, while these plains go on towards north and
east.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
As the wind sweeps over the grass, it appears to be moving, rolling out in no
particular direction. Still, it creates intriguing patterns as it flows out over
the thick, green grass that covers this land like a smooth, soft green carpet.
In all directions the plains stretch out beneath the skies.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The flowing plains spread out like a grey and green carpet of thick and healthy
grass. Small stones can be found here and there lying strewn about, almost
hidden by the tall foliage. No trees or bushes disturb the view, for endless
leagues the land stretches out, rising to the north and revealing another grassy
field with animal tracks running through it.
name: Flowing Plains

desc:
Small spots of tall, rustling grass randomly cut off by little patches of barren
ground, cover these flatlands. Little trails are visible in the tall grass,
evidence of a passing herd of grazing animals. The plains continue in all
directions. To the south, the ground runs lazily downwards, opening the way to a
thick carpet of flowing grass.
name: Plains

desc:
Tall and dense grass covers the ground. Reaching a man's knee, it softly rustles
when the wind blows among its dry stems, creating a music that is slightly
different from that which is heard elsewhere on these plains. In all other
directions the great plains continue, while to the north the land rises, and
shows some low hills, which appear to be good spot for grazing animals to feast
upon.
name: In the Rustling Grass

desc:
The low hills rise from the immense plains, covered with a variety of grasses in
all thinkable shades of green and yellow. Here and there, herbs, low bushes and
other types of low vegetation grow, sheltered from the wind by the low hills.
The grass seems to have been cropped off by herds of grazing animals. The plains
stretch out in all directions.
name: Low Grassy Hills

desc:
The top of the head of an average man would not reach the tips of the stalks
here. The grass is tough and travelling through it is slightly strenuous, save
for the many paths treaded by animals leading in almost every direction. The
ground rises towards a hill to the south.
name: Man-high Grass

desc:
Next to a stand of trees, smaller bushes and other plants grow in abundance. A
few seedlings try to fight for the living space and the sunnier spots, but
without success. To the north, something resembling a path can be recognized.
Very high grass grows everywhere else, blocking large parts of the view, but a
few trees can be seen reaching high towards the sky in the western direction.
name: Dense Foliage

desc:
Old, stocky trees clump together here, offering refreshing shade in summer, and
some protection from the wind in winter. A characteristic bulge on top of the
trunks attracts attention. It is evident that someone had tended these trees
long ago.
name: Cluster of Willows

desc:
The grasslands continue here, with shrubs growing high, high enough to hide a
large animal. West of here the plants are a little shorter. To the east the
plants are even taller. Some trees can be seen in the northerly direction. The
ground rises southwards, towards one of the grass-covered hills that is visible
on the horizon.
name: High Grass

desc:
Here the land rolls out in bigger waves, creating small ridges and low hills
that are covered with the same, tall grass that grows everywhere on the
surrounding plains. The only signs showing that these hills might once have been
taller are a few round boulders that lie strewn about. To the east and south
these small hills continue, while to the north and west the land is flatter.
name: Low Hills

desc:
The low grassy hills provide a welcome change in the otherwise flat landscape
with their small, rolling ridges and sheltered valleys. In these valleys small
bushes and trees grow, hidden away from the wind. To the south and east the land
is flatter, while to the north and west these small hills continue in their
rise.
name: Low Grassy Hills

desc:
The small hills to the north once again spill out to become the low, rolling
flatlands that cover most of this land. Sometimes the wind whispers in the
grass, rustling the long blades and making them sing a strange but, in a way,
beautiful song. The whole plain seems to dance to this tune. To the east and
west the plains stretch out. To the south the land slides downward, giving view
to another extent of grass-filled area.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
The vast grasslands stretch out beneath the giant dome of the sky as far as the
eye can see. On this desolate plain, the sky seems to be the only companion,
displaying a great game as the clouds seem to chase each other from horizon to
horizon. The wind whispers its strange song over the plains, seemingly in search
of an answer. The land rises northwards, allowing a view over these ever-moving
fields.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
This field lies here, like a giant green sea, with waves of shifting and
rustling grass that roll out over the plains. As the tall grass swirls and
moves, it creates strange and beautiful patterns, sometimes revealing the small
animals that can be found beneath the surface of the grass. In all directions
the plain stretches out, but to the south there is a small but rather high
mound, standing out sharply against the sky.
name: A Sea of Grass

desc:
Standing atop the small but tall mound the view is excellent, displaying in all
directions the great plains that extend outwards from this place. To the south
one can spot a giant, lonely oak-tree standing on the plains. And to the west,
beyond even the steel-grey tongue of the Anduin, rise the Misty Mountains. To
the east a small dark band on the horizon marks the Mirkwood. At the top of the
mound, some withered stones stand in memory of some long forgotten hero or
battle.
name: A Small Grassy Mound

desc:
Here, the wind seems to howl rather than whisper its strange song as it rushes
in over the short grass. The flat land provides no shelter from the wind, and
even the grass here seems to have suffered from the strong blasts. Not a single
bush or flower has been able to plant its roots here. The plains stretch out in
all directions, and to the north a small lonely mound stands out against the
sky.
name: Windswept Plains

desc:
The wind rushes in from the north and west here in great, stunning blasts,
howling as it runs over the plains. The only thing able to withstand this seems
